  html_document:
  toc: true

  markdown:

![](images/Football%20Field%20Play%20Calling.jpg){width="540"}

# Introduction:

My goal for this project is to build a machine learning model based on
NFL play calling and how it can decide the outcome of the game for the
team that decides to do that specific play call. We will be using NFL
play calling data from "nflfastr", specifically looking at the 2025-2026
season. I wanted to strictly focus on just this season because of time
constraints with building the project and mostly because I wanted to see
if it functions well with data from just one season compared to
multiple.

Below us we load in the packages and set up our environment.

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

```{R}
#Libraries that I used
library(tidyverse) #primarily utilized tidyverse and tidymodels
library(tidymodels)
library(ggplot2) #Used for our EDA visualizations
library(scales) #Used to cleanly format plot axes 
library(recipes) #Used to build the data preprocessing pipeline
library(ranger) #The high performance engine to build the random forest
library(kknn) #The engine used to run the K-Nearest Neighbors model
library(dplyr) #Used for data manipulation (filter, mutate, summarize, group_by)
library(corrplot) #Used for generating specifically correlaton matrices
library(rpart) #The engine used to build the underlying math for decision tree models
library(rpart.plot) #Used to draw a clean visual of the decision tree model
library(vip) #Used to generate the variable importance plot

```

```{R}


nfl2025playdata <- read.csv("/Users/aryamoghadam/PSTAT 131 - Machine Learning/Final Project Data/play_by_play_2025 .csv")
#summary(nfl2025plays)

#Link: https://nflfastr.com/index.html
```

![](images/Kyle%20Shanahan%20Calling%20Plays.webp){width="542"}

## Aggressive or Conservative Play Calling?

To win a football game in the National Football League, do you go for an
all out aggressive long ball for the win or play it safe and cycle a few
conservative run/short pass plays? This is what this model is here to
answer. In the high-stakes environment of the NFL, offensive
coordinators face a constant, game-defining dilemma: play it safe or go
for broke? This project seeks to mathematically answer a fundamental
question in sports analytics: "**Is conservative play calling or
aggressive play calling more successful in winning big games in the
NFL?**"

## What's the difference?

At first glance, the two philosophies offer vastly different paths to
victory. A ***conservative play*** **calling** approach relies on
predictable, low-risk maneuvers. These involve short passes and steady
run plays designed to gain incremental yardage and safely navigate the
opposing defense. However, this cautious strategy can quietly backfire.
Relying too heavily on small gains can quickly lead to a daunting
long-yardage situation on third or fourth down. Before the offense
realizes it, a drive that felt "safe" stalls out, forcing a punt and
surrendering valuable possession.

But on the other end of the spectrum is the ***aggressive play***
caller. This strategy embraces extreme risk for extreme reward,
characterized by deep downfield passing or boldly attempting to convert
on fourth down rather than punting. Successfully converting an
aggressive fourth-down play keeps the offensive momentum rolling and
applies immense pressure to the defense. Yet, the penalty for failure is
severe.l A failed-fourth down attempt results in a catastrophic turnover
on downs, immediately handing the ball and the momentum back to the
opposing team at the exact spot of the failure.

# Project Roadmap

Now that we know the background and importance of our model, let's
discuss how we are going to build our win predicting model. To determine
which coaching philosophy actually translates to winning football, this
project utilizes comprehensive NFL play-by-play data to build both
predictive and inferential machine learning models. By tracking specific
play types, situational game data (like time and score), and the
ultimate game outcome (Win/Loss), we will use a classification approach
to uncover whether fortune truly favors the bold, or if playing it safe
is the ultimate blueprint for victory. Our goal is to use contextual
predictor variables (such as play style, time remaining, and field
position) to predict a binary class: "won_game," which will be our
response variable detailing whether a team ultimately wins or loses the
match.

We will then perform an 80/20 training/test split on our data, make a
recipe that includes dummy variables, and set folds for the 5-fold
cross-validation we will implement. Basic logistic regression, elastic
net, k-nearest neighbors, decision tree, and random forest models will
all be used to model the training data when we finish the setup.
Whichever model performs the strongest, we will then fit it to our
testing data set, analyze how effective our model can truly be, and
deploy it in a real-world scenario simulator. Without further ado, let's
get play calling!!

![](images/Sean%20McVay%20play%20calling.webp){width="542"}

```{R}
library(plyr)
library(dplyr)


#Cleaning the data first, by filtering for only run and pass plays
clean_pbp <- nfl2025playdata %>%
  filter(play_type %in% c("run", "pass")) %>%
  #Removing QB kneels and spikes which skew conservative metrics
  filter(qb_kneel == 0, qb_spike == 0) %>%
  mutate(
    play_id = as.numeric(play_id),
    down = as.numeric(down),
    ydstogo = as.numeric(ydstogo),
    yardline_100 = as.numeric(yardline_100),
    score_differential = as.numeric(score_differential),
    qtr = as.numeric(qtr),
    posteam_score = as.numeric(posteam_score),
    defteam_score = as.numeric(defteam_score)
  )
  


```

The first step in preparing the raw NFL play-by-play dataset was
isolating the plays that actively reflect offensive decision-making. We
filtered the data to include strictly "run" and "pass" plays.
Furthermore, quarterback kneels and clock-stopping spikes have also been
removed, as these are mandatory clock-management maneuvers that would
artificially skew the metrics for "conservative" play calling. Finally,
a mutation pipeline was applied to convert core contextual variables
(such as `down` `ydstogo`, `field position`, and `score differential`)
into strict numeric data types.

```{R}

#Subsetting the data to only include the requested columns
nfl_subset <- clean_pbp %>%
  select(
    play_id, game_id, home_team, away_team, season_type, yardline_100, 
    qtr, down, ydstogo, yrdln, time, desc, play_type, yards_gained, 
    pass_length, run_location, total_home_score, total_away_score, 
    posteam_score, defteam_score, fourth_down_converted, fourth_down_failed, third_down_converted, 
    third_down_failed, interception, incomplete_pass, fumble_lost, 
    tackled_for_loss, sack, pass_touchdown, rush_touchdown, 
    series_result, play_type_nfl, score_differential, quarter_seconds_remaining, half_seconds_remaining, game_seconds_remaining, posteam, defteam, pass_location
  )

```

The original dataset consisted of hundreds of extra columns. Many of
these were irrelevant to our analysis so we selected only the specific
columns that we needed for our models.

```{R}
#lists the names of the columns that we plan to use from our nfl subset
colnames(nfl_subset)

```

# Morphing the Data

```{r}
late_game_plays <- nfl_subset %>%
  
  mutate(
    score_differential = total_home_score - total_away_score,
    
    game_seconds_remaining =
      suppressWarnings(
        as.numeric(
          as.difftime(as.character(time),
          format="%M:%S",
          units="secs")
        )
      )
  ) %>%
  
  filter(
    qtr >= 4,
    game_seconds_remaining <= 300,
    score_differential >= -8 &
    score_differential <= 8,
    
    !play_type %in% c(
      "no_play",
      "qb_kneel",
      "qb_spike",
      "punt",
      "field_goal"
    )
  ) %>%
  
  mutate(
    
    catastrophic_failure = case_when(
      play_type == "pass" & str_detect(desc,"INTERCEPTED") ~ 1,
      play_type == "pass" & str_detect(desc,"SACK") ~ 1,
      play_type == "run" & str_detect(desc,"FUMBLE") ~ 1,
      down == 4 & yards_gained < ydstogo ~ 1,
      TRUE ~ 0
    ),
    
    massive_success = case_when(
      yards_gained >= 20 ~ 1,
      str_detect(desc,"TOUCHDOWN") ~ 1,
      down == 4 & yards_gained >= ydstogo ~ 1,
      yards_gained >= ydstogo ~ 1,
      TRUE ~ 0
    ),
    
    play_style = case_when(
      play_type == "pass" & pass_length == "deep" ~ "Aggressive",
      play_type == "pass" & pass_length == "short" ~ "Conservative",
      play_type == "run" ~ "Conservative",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    ),
    
    winning_team = case_when(
      total_home_score > total_away_score ~ home_team,
      total_away_score > total_home_score ~ away_team,
      TRUE ~ "Tie"
    ),
    
    won_game = case_when(
      posteam == winning_team ~ "Won Game",
      TRUE ~ "Lost Game"
    ),
    
    play_outcome = case_when(
      massive_success == 1 ~ "Massive Success",
      catastrophic_failure == 1 ~ "Catastrophic Failure",
      TRUE ~ "Standard Play"
    )
    
  ) %>%
  
  select(
    play_id, game_id, posteam, defteam,
    down, ydstogo, yardline_100,
    play_type, pass_length, pass_location,
    run_location,
    catastrophic_failure, massive_success,
    play_style,
    score_differential,
    game_seconds_remaining,
    won_game, winning_team,
    play_outcome, qtr,
    home_team, away_team,
    posteam_score, defteam_score
  ) %>%
  
  drop_na(won_game, catastrophic_failure)
```

## "Late_Game_Plays" dataset

This entire code chunk, `late_game_plays`, is the beating heart or the
absolute engine of our entire data preparation pipeline. It serves three
critical purposes:

1.  **The situational filter:** It strips away all the "noise" of a
    standard football game. By filtering out early quarters, blowouts,
    and clock-killing-kneel-downs, it isolates the exact high-stakes
    do-or-die "crunch time" scenarios our research question is built
    around.

2.  **The Feature Factory:** The raw NFL data doesn't tell us if a play
    was "Aggressive" or if an interception was a "Catastrophic Failure."
    This dataset applies our custom logic to engineer those specific
    predictor and outcome variables from scratch.

3.  **The Machine Learning Foundation**: This is the finalized, pristine
    dataframe that actually gets split into our training and testing
    sets. Every single EDA plot and every predictive model relies
    exclusively on this specific dataset to function.

### How I Put It Together:

-   The first step involved creating two foundational variables.
    `score differential` was calculated to determine the exact point gap
    at the time of the snap. Simultaneously, the raw clock format
    (MM:SS) was converted into `game_seconds_remaining` (a pure numeric
    value) so the machine learning algorithms could process time
    mathematically.

-   My intention for our models is to train them on true high-pressure
    situations in a game. So I filtered the data so that it focuses on
    plays that occurred in the 4th quarter or overtime (`qtr`***\>=
    4**),* with exactly 5 minutes or less remaining
    (`game_seconds_remaining` ***\<= 300**)* and where the score
    differential was within one possession (between -8 and 8 points).

-   Our predictor (`play_style`*)* predicts how plays are categorized
    based on their inherent risk (conservative or aggressive).

-   We created binary flags for `catastrophic_failure` (interceptions,
    fumbles, sacks, and turnover on downs) and ***massive_success*** (20
    yard gains, touchdowns, and critical first down conversions). These
    flags were then grouped into a single ***play_outcome*** variable
    for Exploratory Data Analysis.

-   Our target variable (***won_game***) essentially calculates the
    final outcome of the game and assigned a "Won Game" or "Lost Game"
    label to the team that possessed the ball during that specific play.

I also trimmed our data down to 25 key variables within
`late_game_plays`:

### Identifiers & Team Information

-   `play_id`*:* A unique numerical identifier for the specific play

-   `game_id`*:* A unique identifier for the entire game

-   `posteam`: The team currently possession the football (the offense)

-   `defteam`: The team currently playing defense

-   `home_team`: The designed home team fro the matchup

-   `away_team`*:* The designated away team for the matchup

### Game Context (The situation)

-   `qtr`: The current quarter of the game (filtered to 4th quarter or
    overtime

-   `game_seconds_remaining`: The exact number of seconds left on the
    game clock

-   `score differential`**:** The point difference between the home and
    away team at the time of the snap

-   `posteam_score`***:*** The current score of the team with the ball

-   `defteam_score`***:*** The current score of the defending team

-   `down`***:*** The current down of the play (Ex: 1st, 2nd, 3rd, or
    do-or-die 4th)

-   `ydstogo`: The number of yards required to secure a new first down

-   `yardline_100`***:*** The literal distance to the goal line (100 =
    backed up in own endzone, 0 = opponent's endzone).

### Play Execution (The action)

-   `play_type`***:*** The raw categorization of the play (filtered to
    strictly "run" or "pass)

-   `pass_length`***:*** The intended depth of a pass (Ex: "short" or
    "deep")

-   `pass_location`***:*** The directional target of a pass (left,
    middle, or right)

-   `run_location`***:*** The directional target of a rushing play
    (left, middle, or right)

### Engineered Variables (our custom metrics)

-   `play_style`***:*** Our primary predictor, categorizing the
    offensive decision as "Aggressive" (deep passes) or "Conservative"
    (runs and short passes)

-   `catastrophic_failure`***:*** A binary flag (1 or 0) triggered by
    game-ruining mistakes like sacks, fumbles, interceptions, or a
    turnover on downs.

-   `massive_success`***:*** A binary flag (1 or 0) triggered by
    game-winning execution like 20 yard gains, touchdowns, or critical
    touchdowns.

-   `play_outcome`: A categorical label combining the success/failure
    flags into "Massive Success," "Catastrophic Failure," or "Standard
    Play" for our EDA visuals.

-   `winning_team`***:*** The team that ultimately secured the victory
    at the end of the 4th quarter

-   `won_game`: TARGET VARIABLE! Indicating whether the team possessing
    the ball during this specific play went on to win the game ("Won
    Game" or "Lost Game")

```{R}
#Figure out who won every game
game_winners_table <- nfl_subset %>%
  # Group the data game-by-game
  group_by(game_id) %>%
  # Sort by play_id so the final play of the game is at the very bottom
  arrange(play_id) %>%
  # Grab only the very last row (the final play of the game)
  slice_tail(n = 1) %>%
  # Figure out who had more points when the clock hit 0:00!
  mutate(
    winning_team = case_when(
      posteam_score > defteam_score ~ posteam,
      defteam_score > posteam_score ~ defteam,
      TRUE ~ "Tie"
    )
  ) %>%
  # Keep only the Game ID and the Winning Team
  select(game_id, winning_team) %>%
  ungroup()

```

To train our classification models, we needed a definitive "Win" or
"Loss" outcome for every single play. However, the raw dataset only logs
the score at the exact moment of the play, not the final result of the
game. To solve this, we ultimately created a specialized
(`game_winners_table`). We grouped the data by individual (`game_id`)
and isolated the very last play of each game as the clock hit zero.

```{R}

#View the final results in the console

table(late_game_plays$play_style, late_game_plays$catastrophic_failure)

```

Let's analyze this table these results. Remember, this exclusively
focuses on the plays in the final five minutes of games.

**The columns (0 and 1)**: These represent our `catastrophic_failure`
variable. The 0 column is the number of "Safe" plays (no turnover/sack).
The 1 column is the number of catastrophic failures (a turnover/sack).

**The rows:** These represent our `play_style`.

**The Math:** Teams called 1,751 total Conservative plays in the final 5
minutes of a game (1695 safe + 56 failures). That means only 3.2% of
conservative plays ended in a disaster. Teams called total Aggressive
plays in the final 5 minutes of a game (282 safe + 32 failures). That
means 10.2% of aggressive plays ended in a disaster.

# Exploratory Data Analysis Visuals!!!

## EDA 1: The Risk/Reward of Play Calling

```{R}

# EDA 1: The Risk/Reward Plot

#This line throws out any play labeled as "Other"
late_game_plays %>%
  filter(play_style != "Other") %>%


ggplot(aes(x = play_style, fill = play_outcome)) +
  # position = "fill" makes all bars exactly the same height (100%)
  # This allows us to easily compare percentages side-by-side!
  geom_bar(position = "fill", color = "black", width = 0.6) +
  
  # Format the Y-axis as percentages
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  
  # Assign custom colors: Green for success, Red for failure, Gray for standard
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Massive Success" = "#2ca25f", 
                               "Standard Play" = "#cccccc", 
                               "Catastrophic Failure" = "#f03b20")) +
  labs(
    title = "EDA 1: The Risk vs. Reward of Play Calling",
    subtitle = "Comparing Success and Failure Rates by Play Style",
    x = "Play Style",
    y = "Percentage of Plays",
    fill = "Play Outcome"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    # Make the X-axis labels a bit bigger and bolder
    axis.text.x = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12) 
  )
#this first plot adds standard plays




#What if we wanted to filter out the standard plays?

#Filter out the standard plays:
extreme_outcomes_data <- late_game_plays %>%
  filter(play_outcome %in% c("Massive Success", "Catastrophic Failure"),
         play_style != "Other")


#Build the Extreme Risk/Reward Matrix
ggplot(extreme_outcomes_data, aes(x = play_style, fill = play_outcome)) +
  # 100% stacked bar chart
  geom_bar(position = "fill", color = "black", width = 0.6) +
  
  # Format Y-axis as percentages
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  
  # Keep our exact same Red and Green colors!
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Massive Success" = "#2ca25f", 
                               "Catastrophic Failure" = "#f03b20")) +
  labs(
    title = "EDA 1b: The Extremes of Play Calling",
    subtitle = "Success vs. Failure Rates (Excluding Standard Plays)",
    x = "Play Style",
    y = "Percentage of Extreme Outcomes",
    fill = "Play Outcome"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12) 
  )

```

Here we have two plots (one of them including standard plays to sort of
visualize how often they occur compared to aggressive/conservative
plays, and another just includes exclusively aggressive/conservative
plays). Looking at these plots, these visuals perfectly capture the
dilemma facing NFL play-callers in the late stages of a game. As shown
on the left, an aggressive play-style successfully generates a higher
rate of 'Massive Successes'. However, it simultaneously exposes the team
to a significantly higher rate of 'Catastrophic Failures'. Conversely,
conservative play-calling minimizes the chance of a game-ending
turnover, keeping the play within the 'Standard/Safe' parameters.
Ultimately, this proves that aggressive passing is a high-variance
strategy, while running and short passing are low-variance strategies.
Aggressive plays, while risky, offer a much more balanced ratio of
extreme rewards to extreme punishments.

## EDA 2: Does pulling off an aggressive play actually win games?

```{R}
# EDA 2: Win Percentage by Play Style and Play Outcome
ggplot(late_game_plays, aes(x = play_style, fill = won_game)) +
  # 100% stacked bars to show win probability
  geom_bar(position = "fill", color = "black", width = 0.6) +
  
  # facet_wrap splits the plot into 3 panels based on what happened on the play!
  facet_wrap(~ play_outcome) +
  
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  
  # Keeping our intuitive Green = Win, Red = Loss colors
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Won Game" = "#2ca25f", "Lost Game" = "#f03b20")) +
  
  labs(
    title = "EDA 2: Does 'Pulling It Off' Actually Win Games?",
    subtitle = "Comparing Game Win % between Aggressive and Conservative calls",
    x = "Play Style Called",
    y = "Game Win Percentage",
    fill = "Final Game Result"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    # Make the panel headers bold
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10),
    # Angle the X-axis text slightly so Aggressive and Conservative don't overlap
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, face = "bold") 
  )
```

Looking at the "catastrophic failure" panel, the takeaway is that no
matter if a coach is being aggressive or conservative, a catastrophic
failure (a turnover, a sack, or a failed fourth down) in the late game
is absolutely devastating to their chances of winning. The penalty for
failure is a near-guaranteed loss.

Where things get interesting is under the "massive success" panel (where
big yardage plays are converted or first downs are converted
successfully), pulling off a massive success drastically increases the
win probability. But if we look closely at the two bars: if the
conservative massive success (like a huge run or a gritty 4th down
conversion) leads to a similar or higher win percentage than an
aggressive massive success (like a deep pass), it proves a massive
point. It means that grinding out yards on the ground while keeping the
clock moving is just as, if not more, valuable than a flashy deep bomb.

Looking at the standard play panel, which includes plays that don't
include huge gains and no turnovers, the takeaway from this graph is
that if the conservative bar has more green here, it means that playing
it safe, protecting the football, and grinding out standard yardage is
generally a more successful baseline strategy for securing a win than
throwing standard passes.

So the takeaway from this plot sort of answers the question: "Is
conservative play calling or aggressive play calling more successful in
winning big games?" While aggressive plays might generate a higher raw
volume of massive successes, the risk is rarely worth the reward. The
data proves that catastrophic failures almost guarantee a loss.
Therefore, the statistically superior strategy in late-game situations
is to play conservatively, protect the football, and minimize the risk
of a game-ending mistake."

## EDA 3: The Blueprint for Winning (Correlation Matrix)

```{R}

#Prepare the data
# 1. Prepare the data (The Ironclad Version)
corr_data <- late_game_plays %>%
  ungroup() %>% # Removes any hidden groupings that might confuse R
  mutate(
    Win_Game = ifelse(won_game == "Won Game", 1, 0),
    Score_Diff = as.numeric(score_differential),
    Seconds_Left = as.numeric(game_seconds_remaining),
    Yards_to_Go = as.numeric(ydstogo),
    # Safely extract the 1s and 0s regardless of if they are factors or text
    Failure = as.numeric(as.character(catastrophic_failure)),
    Success = as.numeric(as.character(massive_success))
  ) %>%
  select(Win_Game, Score_Diff, Seconds_Left, Yards_to_Go, Failure, Success) %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  # THE ULTIMATE HAMMER: This absolutely forces every remaining column to be a pure number
  mutate(across(everything(), as.numeric)) %>%
  # Strip away any 'tibble' formatting just in case cor() is being overly sensitive
  as.data.frame()

# 2. Calculate the mathematical correlations
cor_matrix <- cor(corr_data, use = "complete.obs")


# 3. Reshape the matrix so ggplot can draw it as a grid
cor_data_long <- cor_matrix %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column(var = "Var1") %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -Var1, names_to = "Var2", values_to = "Correlation")


#Build the Dot Matrix Plot (Clean Version)
ggplot(cor_data_long, aes(x = Var1, y = Var2)) +
  # geom_point creates the dots! 
  geom_point(aes(size = abs(Correlation), color = Correlation)) +
  
  # (The geom_text layer has been completely removed from here!)
  
  # Color scale: Red for negative, Blue for positive
  scale_color_gradient2(low = "#f03b20", mid = "gray90", high = "#2c7fb8", 
                       midpoint = 0, limit = c(-1, 1), name = "Correlation") +
  
  # Manually control the minimum and maximum size of the dots
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(4, 16), guide = "none") +
  
  labs(
    title = "EDA 3: The Blueprint for Winning",
    subtitle = "Correlation Dot Matrix of Late-Game Factors and Win Probability",
    x = "",
    y = ""
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, face = "bold"),
    axis.text.y = element_text(face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey80", linetype = "dashed") 
  )

```

Here is a correlation heatmap describing which variables/predictors are
the most effective when it comes to winning games. This chart will be a
grid of squares ranging from -1 to 1.

Looking specifically at the row labeled `Win_Game`, the `Score_Diff`
square will have a shade of blue (meaning a positive correlation). It
simply means that having a lead (a positive score differential) when you
snap the ball highly correlates with winning the game. Where `Win_Game`
and the `Failure` square intersect, this will most certainly include a
shade of red (a negative correlation). Mathematically, as those failures
go up, the chances of winning will plummet. Having `success` and winning
the game is most certainly a positive correlation because converting on
those first downs and scoring on touchdowns definitely gives the team a
chance at winning the game.

`Seconds_Left` and `Win_Game` have a pretty high positive correlation
because having 50 seconds left on the clock does not necessarily mean
that the team wins or loses. But it entirely depends on the score
differential, which is the ultimate dictator of the game's outcome.

Seeing `yards_to_go` and `Success` having a negative correlation makes
sense. Because a negative correlation means that as one number goes up,
the other number goes down. If `yards_to_go` is very low (for instance a
3rd & 1 situation), it only takes a 1-yard plunge to trigger a "Success"
in our dataset. NFL teams almost always convert these short-yardage
situations at a very high rate. But when `yards_to_go` is very high, the
offense has to magically pull off a 15 yard play in order to keep the
drive alive. The success rate for this is naturally incredibly low.

## EDA 4: The Down Dilemma

#### Does the current down significantly change the probability of a catastrophic failure?

```{R}
library(dplyr)

# EDA 4: Down vs Failure Rate
eda_down_data <- late_game_plays %>%
  # BULLETPROOF FIX 1: Explicitly keep ONLY valid football downs
  # This automatically throws away NAs, "NA"s, or any other garbage data
  dplyr::filter(down %in% c(1, 2, 3, 4, "1", "2", "3", "4")) %>%
  
  dplyr::group_by(down) %>%
  
  # BULLETPROOF FIX 2: Catch the failure whether it's stored as text OR a number!
  dplyr::summarize(
    total_plays = dplyr::n(),
    #Using %in% catches the label whether it's a factor "1", numeric 1, or text!
    failure_count = sum(catastrophic_failure %in% c(1, "1", "Failure", "Yes"), na.rm = TRUE),
    failure_rate = failure_count / total_plays,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# The Plot
ggplot(eda_down_data, aes(x = factor(down), y = failure_rate)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "#2c7fb8", width = 0.6) +
  
  # Add the percentage text on top of the bars!
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(failure_rate, accuracy = 0.1)),
            vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold", size = 5) +
  
  # Expand the top of the Y-axis slightly so the text doesn't get cut off
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) + 
  labs(
    title = "EDA 4: The Pressure of the Downs",
    subtitle = "Does the current down significantly 
    change the probability of a catastrophic failure?",
    x = "Down",
    y = "Catastrophic Failure Rate"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank() 
  )

```

What we can see from this graph is how identical the first, second, and
third down catastrophic failure percentage rates are (which includes
fumbles, interceptions, or sacks). They are actually quite rare since
the team on offense still has the safety net of "the next down". That
means they don't have to go for desperation plays. But it peaks when it
reaches 4th down because almost all the time a team will turn it over on
downs.

But let's say we exclude the idea of a team failing on fourth down and
only include the idea of turnovers, sacks, and interceptions. How would
our plot look like there?

```{R}

# EDA 4b: Pure Turnovers & Sacks by Down
eda_down_data_pure <- nfl_subset %>%
  # 1. Recreate your exact late-game filters so the data matches perfectly
  dplyr::mutate(
    score_differential = as.numeric(total_home_score) - as.numeric(total_away_score),
    game_seconds_remaining = suppressWarnings(as.numeric(as.difftime(as.character(time), format="%M:%S", units="secs")))
  ) %>%
  dplyr::filter(
    qtr >= 4,
    game_seconds_remaining <= 300,
    score_differential >= -8 & score_differential <= 8,
    !play_type %in% c("no_play", "qb_kneel", "qb_spike", "punt", "field_goal"),
    down %in% c(1, 2, 3, 4, "1", "2", "3", "4")
  ) %>%
  
  # 2. THE NEW RULE: Only Sacks, INTs, and Fumbles!
  dplyr::mutate(
    pure_failure = dplyr::case_when(
      play_type == "pass" & stringr::str_detect(desc, "INTERCEPTED") ~ 1,
      play_type == "pass" & stringr::str_detect(desc, "SACK") ~ 1,
      play_type == "run" & stringr::str_detect(desc, "FUMBLE") ~ 1,
      TRUE ~ 0
    )
  ) %>%
  
  # 3. Group and summarize with bulletproof dplyr formatting
  dplyr::group_by(down) %>%
  dplyr::summarize(
    total_plays = dplyr::n(),
    failure_count = sum(pure_failure == 1, na.rm = TRUE),
    failure_rate = failure_count / total_plays,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# The Plot
ggplot(eda_down_data_pure, aes(x = factor(down), y = failure_rate)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "#f03b20", width = 0.6) + # Red for Danger!
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(failure_rate, accuracy = 0.1)),
            vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold", size = 5) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.20))) + 
  labs(
    title = "EDA 4b: The Pure Error Rate by Down",
    subtitle = "Excluding 'Turnover on Downs' to isolate Sacks, INTs, and Fumbles",
    x = "Down",
    y = "Pure Failure Rate (Sacks/INTs/Fumbles)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank() 
  )






```

What we can take away from this plot is it's failure rate is greatly
affected when looking at fourth down, because there is still a greater
chance that a team who goes for it on fourth down throws an interception
or gets sacked. And this is because of the magnitude of pressure it
takes to convert on a fourth down, knowing how it's now or never.

## EDA 5: Field Position Geography

#### Are teams more likely to make a catastrophic mistake when backed up against their own endzone, or when knocking on the door of the opponent's endzone?

```{R}

#1. Filter out the standard plays
density_extremes_data <- late_game_plays %>%
  filter(play_outcome %in% c("Massive Success", "Catastrophic Failure"))

# 2. Build the focused Density Plot
ggplot(density_extremes_data, aes(x = yardline_100, fill = play_outcome, color = play_outcome)) +
  # geom_density creates the smooth mountain shapes
  geom_density(alpha = 0.5, linewidth = 1) +
  
  # Reverse the X axis so 100 (Own Endzone) is on the left, and 0 (Scoring) is on the right
  scale_x_reverse(breaks = seq(100, 0, by = -10)) +
  
  # Keeping our Green and Red colors!
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Massive Success" = "#2ca25f", 
                               "Catastrophic Failure" = "#f03b20")) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Massive Success" = "#2ca25f", 
                                "Catastrophic Failure" = "#f03b20")) +
  labs(
    title = "EDA 5: Where Do Extreme Plays Happen?",
    subtitle = "Field Position Density: Massive Successes vs. Catastrophic Failures",
    x = "Yards to Goal (100 = Own Endzone, 0 = Opponent Endzone)",
    y = "Density (Concentration of Plays)",
    fill = "Play Outcome",
    color = "Play Outcome"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.title.x = element_text(face = "bold", margin = margin(t = 10)) 
  )

```

This cool looking plot quite literally resembles a football field! From
100 to 0 (the team on offense tries to move down the field and gain
yards towards the direction of the opponent endzone).

The density plot maps the geographical distribution of extreme outcomes,
revealing a fascinating divergence between success and failure. "Massive
Success" (the green curve) heavily concentrate between the 50 and 70
yard lines, which logically tracks. As generating a 20+ yard play
requires sufficient open field. However, "Catastrophic Failures" (the
red curve) peak significantly further down the field, cresting around
the 30-40 yard line. This area represents a zone where teams are often
too close to punt (punting is used to surrender possession to the other
team if the team is too far away from scoring a field goal or a
touchdown. Usually on 4th down this is pulled off. A punter usually
kicks the ball as far as he can down the field to maximize field
position.) Consequently, in late game situations, offenses are forced to
attempt risky 4th down conversions or throw deep into condensed
coverage, causing the failure rate to spike as a result.

This proves that field position itself acts as a multiplier for risk.
Therefore, calling aggressive, high-variance plays when backed up
against one's own endzone is statistically the most dangerous decision
an offensive coordinator can make.

## EDA 6: Yards to Go Boxplot

## How does the required 'yards to go' differ between aggressive and conservative play calls

```{R}

# EDA 6: Yards to Go Boxplot
late_game_plays %>%
  filter(play_style != "Other") %>%

  ggplot(aes(x = play_style, y = ydstogo, fill = play_style)) +
  # geom_boxplot builds the boxes, alpha makes them slightly transparent
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, color = "black", outlier.color = "red", outlier.alpha = 0.5) +
  
  # Keeping our Aggressive (Red) and Conservative (Blue) colors
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Aggressive" = "#f03b20", "Conservative" = "#2c7fb8")) +
  
  # Y-axis breaks every 5 yards to make it easy to read
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 40, by = 5)) +
  
  labs(
    title = "EDA 6: Does Distance Dictate Aggression?",
    subtitle = "Yards to Go vs. Play Style Called in the Late Game",
    x = "Play Style Called",
    y = "Yards to Go (for 1st Down)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

```

Looking at this boxplot, we can see that the thick black median line for
both the red and blue boxes is sitting exactly at the 10-yard mark and
the top of both boxes stop right there too. Because the vast majority of
plays in an NFL game happen with exactly 10 yards to go (1st & 10). And
a result, it creates a massive gravitational pull in the data.

Looking at the short yardage situations (which would be the bottom line
of the boxes, representing the 25th percentile of the data). The bottom
of the conservative box stretches way down to about 3 or 4 yards. Which
means that the team is in a short yardage situation in order to get the
first down (like 3rd & 3 or 2nd & 1). Coaches tend to overwhelmingly
lean on conservative play calls (like runs or quick slants) to move the
chains. However, the aggressive box stops much higher, around 7 yards.
This shows that coaches very rarely dial up deep, aggressive shots when
they only need a few yards to get the first down.

But what are those dots located way above the boxes? Those that stretch
out to 20, 30 and even 40 yards (basically like a 2nd & 20, or 3rd & 30
situation). These are our outliers of the data, located within both play
call categories. The red dots located in the aggressive category high
represent the "Desperation Deep Ball," which is ultimately an aggressive
play because they absolutely have to get 20 yards. The blue dots located
in the conservative category represent a play being drawn from 3rd & 25
or 2nd & 20. At that point coaches have completely given up on trying to
get the first down and just running a conservative play to get better
punting position to avoid a turnover.

## EDA 7: The "Danger Zone" Faceted Scatterplot

#### Does field position and yards-to-go actually dictate risk, or does the offensive coordinator's play style override everything else?

```{R}

# EDA 7: Faceted Scatterplot

late_game_plays %>%
  filter(play_style != "Other") %>%
  
ggplot(aes(x = yardline_100, y = ydstogo, color = factor(catastrophic_failure))) +
  # geom_jitter slightly shakes the dots so we can see all of them!
  geom_jitter(alpha = 0.6, width = 1, height = 0.5, size = 2) +
  # facet_wrap splits the plot into two side-by-side charts based on play style
  facet_wrap(~ play_style) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("0" = "#2c7fb8", "1" = "#f03b20"), 
                     labels = c("Safe", "Failure")) +
  labs(
    title = "EDA 7: The 'Danger Zone' by Play Style",
    subtitle = "Field Position vs. Yards to Go",
    x = "Distance to Goal (Yardline_100)",
    y = "Yards to Go",
    color = "Outcome"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14) # Makes the Aggressive/Conservative labels bold
  )

```

The faceted scatter plot reveals the geographical 'Danger zone' of
late-game play calling. While the sheer volume of plays falls under the
Conservative category, the Aggressive panel displays a visibly higher
density of Catastrophic failures (red dots). Specifically, when teams
attempt Aggressive plays with 10 or more yards to go, the risk of a
game-altering turnover or sack increases noticeably across almost all
field positions. This visual evidence suggests that Aggressive
play-calling introduces a high-variance risk that Conservative
play-calling successfully avoids.

## Setting Up Models

### Setting up training/testing split

Now that we're done looking at all those cool, colorful, and informative
visualizations, let's now set up our models! But first, we need to
train/test our data.

Setting up the training/testing split with stratified sampling, followed
up by the "universal recipe". We are looking at a classification
problem, because we're predicting whether the play was successful or did
it fail (like a yes/no question).

For stratified sampling, we do specifically an 80/20 split, 80% of the
training set and 20% of the testing set) and from that 80/20 split, the
exact same percentage consist of "Yes" and "No" failures. Before passing
the data into the machine learning algorithms, the target variable
(`won_game` and `catastrophic_failure`) were converted to factors to
enable classification mapping. To evaluate the models effectively, the
perfectly cleaned dataset was partitioned using an 80/20 train-test
split.

```{R}

library(dplyr)

#We first convert catastrophic_failure to a proper factor
late_game_plays <- late_game_plays %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    #Use as.factor() to let R handle the existing text/numbers
    catastrophic_failure = as.factor(catastrophic_failure),
    won_game = as.factor(won_game),
    score_differential = as.numeric(score_differential),
    ydstogo = as.numeric(ydstogo),
    yardline_100 = as.numeric(yardline_100),
    down = as.numeric(down)
    )

#Splitting the data:
set.seed(2025) 
#Creating the 80/20 split, stratified by our target variable
data_split <- initial_split(late_game_plays, prop = 0.80, strata = won_game)


#Extract the training and testing datasets
train_data <- training(data_split)
test_data <- testing(data_split)

#Clean the training data here
train_data <- train_data %>%
tidyr::drop_na(
  won_game, catastrophic_failure, play_style, score_differential, game_seconds_remaining, ydstogo, yardline_100, down
)

train_data_clean <- train_data %>%
  dplyr::mutate(dplyr::across(where(is.character), ~na_if(.x, ""))) %>%
  # Convert Inf/NaN to NA so drop_na can see them
  dplyr::mutate(dplyr::across(where(is.numeric), ~if_else(is.finite(.x), .x, NA_real_)))
  




# Convert problematic variables to numeric (not integer)
#train_data <- train_data %>%
 # mutate(
  #  score_differential = as.numeric(score_differential),
  #  ydstogo = as.numeric(ydstogo),
  #  yardline_100 = as.numeric(yardline_100),
  ## ) %>%

#Drop rows with missing values ONLY in relevant variables
 # tidyr::drop_na(
    #won_game, catastrophic_failure, play_style, 
 #   score_differential, game_seconds_remaining, 
     # ydstogo, yardline_100, down
 # )


  
  
#Verifying no NaNs or Infs remain (using dplyr:: to prevent errors)
#train_data_clean %>%
  #dplyr::summarise(dplyr::across(everything(), ~ sum(is.nan(.x) | is.infinite(.x))))



#I set the seed value to 2025, so that the folds are identical every time I run it
set.seed(2025) 
#this gives us the exact same random split every time that I run the split
data_folds <- vfold_cv(train_data_clean, v = 5, strata = won_game) #used for resampling the training data to get better estimate of testing error, without having to touch the testing error



#Verifying that the split worked:
nrow(train_data_clean)
nrow(test_data)



#Create the confusion matrix at the very end and use only one of them for the best possible model
#I need to fit it into the folds and looking at the cross validation error, using tune_grid(), fit_resamples() and collect_metrics()

```

Because NFL game outcomes can be imbalanced, stratified sampling
(`strata = won_game)` was utilized during this split to guarantee that
both the training and testing sets contained the exact same proportion
of wins and losses.

To safely tune the models without exposing them to the testing data, the
training set was divided into 5 distinct folds using V-fold
cross-validation. This re-sampling technique allows the models to
iteratively train and validate themselves, providing a robust estimate
of out-of-sample performance while preventing overfitting.

Doing stratified sampling for cross-validation is useful because it
ensures that the proportion of the outcome variable
(`catastrophic_failure`) is kept consistent across our training set, our
test set, and every single one of our cross-validation folds.

## Recipe Building/Data Cleaning:

```{R}
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

# 1. Fully clean and prep the dataset BEFORE splitting!
late_game_plays_clean <- late_game_plays %>%
  # Fix the types
  dplyr::mutate(
    catastrophic_failure = as.factor(catastrophic_failure),
    won_game = as.factor(won_game),
    score_differential = as.numeric(score_differential),
    ydstogo = as.numeric(ydstogo),
    yardline_100 = as.numeric(yardline_100),
    down = as.numeric(down)
  ) %>%
  # Replace blank strings with NA
  dplyr::mutate(dplyr::across(where(is.character), ~na_if(.x, ""))) %>%
  # Convert Inf/NaN to NA
  dplyr::mutate(dplyr::across(where(is.numeric), ~if_else(is.finite(.x), .x, NA_real_))) %>%
  # DROP THE NAs HERE
  tidyr::drop_na(
    won_game, catastrophic_failure, play_style, 
    score_differential, game_seconds_remaining, 
    ydstogo, yardline_100, down
  )
#We run drop_na() on the entire dataset before doing the splitting process
#Because by doing this, we completely throw any invalid plays in the trash

# 2. NOW split the perfectly clean data
set.seed(2025) 
data_split <- initial_split(late_game_plays_clean, prop = 0.80, strata = won_game)

# 3. Extract train and test sets (they are now naturally clean!)
train_data <- training(data_split)
test_data <- testing(data_split)

# 4. Create the folds for tuning
set.seed(2025) 
data_folds <- vfold_cv(train_data, v = 5, strata = won_game)


#Verifying that the split worked:
nrow(train_data)
nrow(test_data)


```

We create the universal recipe here. For a mixed dataset like the one
that we have (some categorical data like `play_style` and some numerical
data like `ydstogo`), a universal recipe typically requires three
specific steps:

1.  **Dummy variables**: Machine learning models only understand
    numbers. `step_dummy()` turns categories (like "Aggressive" /
    "Conservative") into binary 1s and 0s behind the scenes.

2.  **Normalization**: Columns like `yardline_100` go from 1 to 99,
    while `ydstogo` might only go from 1 to 10. `step_normalize()` puts
    all of the numbers on the same mathematical scale so the model
    doesn't get confused by the big numbers.

3.  **Zero Variance Filter**: `step_zv()` removes any columns that
    contain only one value (which provide zero predictive power).

```{r}

#train_data$won_game <- as.character(train_data$won_game)
#because classification models require our outcome to be a factor.


library(themis) # Make sure this is loaded for SMOTE

nfl_recipe <- recipe(
  won_game ~ catastrophic_failure + play_style + score_differential + game_seconds_remaining + 
             ydstogo + yardline_100 + down, # Added 'down' since it caused errors earlier
  data = train_data_clean
) %>%
  
  #Handle actual NAs in numeric predictors first
  #step_naomit(all_predictors(), all_outcomes()) %>%
  #the model crashes if we have this line
  
  
    #Convert any lingering strings to factors first 
  step_string2factor(all_nominal_predictors()) %>%
  
  
  #Handle "New" or "Unknown" categories in categorical predictors
  # This prevents the "New levels in game_id" style errors
  step_novel(all_nominal_predictors()) %>%
  step_unknown(all_nominal_predictors()) %>%
  
  #Clean up rare categories (lumping them into "other")
  step_other(play_style, threshold = 0.05) %>%
  step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors()) %>%
  
  #Remove columns with only one value (zero-variance)
  step_zv(all_predictors()) %>%
  
  #Normalize numeric data (crucial for KNN and elastic net)
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors()) %>%
  
  #Balance the dataset with SMOTE (must be the very last step)
  step_smote(won_game)
```

```{R}
#Keeping track of the missing data
#Calculate the exact number of rows dropped
original_row_count <- nrow(late_game_plays)
clean_row_count <- nrow(late_game_plays_clean)
rows_dropped <- original_row_count - clean_row_count

#Print the official statement for the report
cat("Out of", original_row_count, "total late-game plays,", 
    rows_dropped, "rows were dropped due to missing or invalid data, leaving", 
    clean_row_count, "perfectly clean plays for the model to train and test on.")

```

Within our data cleaning pipeline, we tackled the missing data in three
steps:

1.  We unmasked the hidden NA's. We used na_if `na_if(.x, "")` to find
    blank text strings and force them to become official NA's.

2.  Mathematical Scrubbing: We used if_else `if_else`
    `(is.finite(.x), .x, NA_real)` to find impossible math outputs (like
    NaN or Inf that sometimes happen when calculating clock times) and
    forced them to become NA.

3.  Targeted Deletion: We used `drop_na(...)` specifically on our core
    predictors and outcomes (`won_game`, `play_style`, `ydstogo`, etc.).

The raw NFL play-by-play dataset contained instances of missing data,
blank character strings, and invalid numeric calculations (such as NaN
or Inf values generated during time-remaining conversions). Handling
missing data was a critical step prior to the initial data split to
ensure the model was trained strictly on valid, factual game states.

In football analytics, a play's context is an absolute factual event;
mathematically estimating a missing "`yardline_100`" or "`ydstogo`"
value would introduce fabricated scenarios into the model and corrupt
the situational context.

Missing data was handled using targeted Complete Case Analysis. First,
all blank strings were coerced into NA values, and all infinite or
non-numeric values were coerced into "`NA_real_`" . Finally,
"`tidyr::drop_na()`" was applied exclusively to the core predictors and
outcome variables required for the models (`won_game`,
`catastrophic_failure`, `play_style`, `score_differential`,
`game_seconds_remaining`, `ydstogo`, `yardline_100`, and `down`). Any
play missing these critical contextual data points was entirely removed
from the dataset prior to the 80/20 train-test split, ensuring that both
the training recipe and final testing metrics were built on a perfectly
clean foundation. Furthermore, the `step_unknown()` function was
utilized in the recipe to gracefully handle any unforeseen missing
categorical levels during cross validation.

# Modeling Time!!

## Non-tuned Logistic Regression:

Let's start things off with a basic non-tuned logistic regression model.
And then we look at an elastic net after this:

```{R}
library(tidymodels)

basic_log_spec <- logistic_reg() %>%
  set_engine("glm") %>%
  set_mode("classification")

basic_log_workflow <- workflow() %>%
  add_recipe(nfl_recipe) %>%
  add_model(basic_log_spec)

basic_log_resamples <- fit_resamples(
  basic_log_workflow,
  resamples = data_folds,
  metrics = metric_set(roc_auc, accuracy),
  control = control_resamples(save_pred = TRUE)
)

collect_metrics(basic_log_resamples)
```

### Extracting the basic logistic regression model

```{R}
# Since there are no tuning parameters, we just use the original workflow directly!
basic_log_final_fit <- last_fit(
  basic_log_workflow, 
  split = data_split
)

# View the final test metrics for the basic logistic regression
collect_metrics(basic_log_final_fit)

```

A standard logistic regression model is linear. It essentially tries to
draw a straight simple line through our data to separate the wins from
the losses. And ultimately it looks at each variable in isolation.
Football is highly situational however. A conservative play style on 1st
down at a team's own 20 yard line means something completely different
than a conservative play style on 4th down at the opponent's 5 yard
line.

What we can see from our results is it's accuracy is at 65.4%. And an
ROC_AUC value of 0.714. That means our model can't handle the complex
"If it's the 4th quarter AND are down by 4, then aggressive is better"
logic.

## Elastic Net:

Creating a Logistic Regression (Elastic Net) Model. We need to use an
engine called `glmnet`

By using `glmnet`, we unlock two new dials that we can tune to prevent
over-fitting:

1.  `penalty`: How harshly the model should penalize variables that
    aren't actually useful (This helps ignore the "noise" within the
    data)

2.  `mixture`: The balance between two different styles of penalties
    (Lasso vs. Ridge)

```{R}
#Defining the model specification
elastic_net_spec <- logistic_reg(penalty = tune(), mixture = tune()) %>%
  #we use "glmnet" so we can tune the penalty (regularization) to prevent overfitting
  set_engine("glmnet") %>%
  set_mode("classification")


#Bundling it into a workflow
elastic_net_workflow <- workflow() %>%
  add_model(elastic_net_spec) %>%
  add_recipe(nfl_recipe)

    

#Now we create a tuning grid
#This essentially gives the model 50 different penalty values to try out
#penalty_grid <- grid_regular(penalty(range = c(-5, 1)), levels = 50)
 
    
 
#Setting up a grid of penalty and mixture values to try
# (glmnet requires these specific tuning parameters)
    elastic_net_grid <- grid_regular(penalty(),
                                     mixture(),
                                     levels = 5)
  

#Tuning the model here (which is where the magic happens)
set.seed(2025)
elastic_net_tune_results <- tune_grid(
  elastic_net_workflow,
  resamples = data_folds,
  grid = elastic_net_grid
)

#View the results
collect_metrics(elastic_net_tune_results)

```

### Extracting the best penalty and mixture combination:

```{R}

#Extract the best penalty and mixture combination
best_elastic_params <- select_best(elastic_net_tune_results, metric = "roc_auc")

#Plug those best parameters back into the workflow
elastic_final_workflow <- finalize_workflow(
  elastic_net_workflow,
  best_elastic_params
)

#Fit the finalized workflow to the testing data
elastic_final_fit <- last_fit(
  elastic_final_workflow,
  split = data_split
)

#View the final test metrics for the Elastic Net
collect_metrics(elastic_final_fit)


```

A mean score that lies between 0.60 and 0.70 is decent. But if it's 0.70
and 0.80 that would mean it's strong and really good for real-world
sports data. The 'n' column confirms that each of these means was
calculated across all 5 of our cross-validation folds. The tuned elastic
net model gave us an ROC AUC amount of approximately 0.667. But the
basic logistic regression model gave us an ROC AUC amount of about
0.6669. Tuning the penalties didn't really improve the model all that
much. Since our data has hit the "linear ceiling".

Remembering our main question that we are trying to find an answer for:
"Is conservative or aggressive play calling more successful in winning
big games?" Linear models (like logistic regression and elastic net) try
to give a single, rigid, straight line answer to that question. It might
try to decide that "aggressive is always better and more impactful" and
apply that rule across the board. But in the NFL, the success of a
conservative or aggressive play call is entirely dependent on the
context, the quarter, the score differential, and the yard line.

From what we found out, both our basic logistic regression and elastic
net models were suffering from underfitting. They simply could not
capture the complex, overlapping realities of NFL play calling.
Regularizing the linear model via an Elastic net provided negligible
improvements over the baseline Logistic regression. This indicates that
the limitations of the linear models were not due to high variance or
multicolinearity, but rather the inherently non-linear and conditional
nature of late-game football strategy.

```{R}
library(ggplot2)

# Generate the autoplot for the Logistic Regression tuning results
#Essentially the elastic net model
# Replace 'log_tune_results' with whatever you named your tuning object!
autoplot(elastic_net_tune_results, metric = "roc_auc")

```

Looking at the elastic net tuning plot demonstrates that applying
regularization failed to improve the model's performance. It tells a
very specific, yet somewhat dramatic story about how linear models
struggle to understand the complexities of an NFL football game. The
Y-axis (roc_auc) shows the model's "grade." The highest points on this
graph hover right around 0.66 to 0.67. The X-axis (amount of
regularization) represents the penalty. On the far left, the model is
basically unpunished (it acts like a standard logistic regression). As
we move to the right, we are applying a more stricter and stricter
mathematical penalty, forcing the model to shrink or drop variables.

The red line (0.00) is pure Ridge regression (it shrinks variables but
rarely drops them). The purple line (1.00) is pure Lasso regression (it
acts like a bouncer, aggressively kicking variables entirely ouf of the
model if they aren't useful).

This further confirms that NFL play-calling success requires the
non-linear, contextual mapping. And those would be provided by the tree
models which we'll look below.

## Tuned K-Nearest Neighbors model:

Next we will experiment using a tuned K-nearest neighbors model. It's
very intuitive for sports analytics. When it tries to predict if a play
will fail, it looks at the dataset, finds the 5 plays that are the most
mathematically similar to the current play (same yard line, same down,
same distance, same play style), and basically takes a vote. If 4 out of
the 5 similar plays were safe, then it deems a prediction that the new
play will be safe too.

One important rule with KNN models is the numeric data MUST be
normalized. KNN works by calculating the literal, geometric distance
between data points. If `game_seconds_remaining` is a massive number and
`score_differential` is a tiny number (like 3), the math is going to
completely ignore the score and only care about the clock. So we use
`step_normalize()` in our recipe to shrink all numeric variables down to
the exact same scale so that they have an equal voice.

```{R}
#Defining the KNN model specification
#We tell the model to tune the 'neighbors' parameter
knn_spec <- nearest_neighbor(neighbors = tune()) %>%
  set_engine("kknn") %>%
  set_mode("classification")

#Bundling it into a workflow
knn_workflow <- workflow() %>%
  add_recipe(nfl_recipe) %>%
  add_model(knn_spec)

#Creating the tuning grid
#We test 25 different values for k, ranging from 1 to 50
knn_grid <- grid_regular(neighbors(range = c(1, 50)), levels = 25)


#Tuning the model across our folds, which could take a bit of time
knn_tune_results <- tune_grid(
  knn_workflow,
  resamples = data_folds,
  grid = knn_grid,
  metrics = metric_set(roc_auc, accuracy)
)

#Show the final scorecard
#show_best(knn_tune_results, metric = "roc_auc")

print(knn_tune_results)


# This unpacks the box and shows us the average scores for all 50 values of K
collect_metrics(knn_tune_results)

# This will specifically print out a leaderboard of the top 5 best values of K
show_best(knn_tune_results, metric = "roc_auc")

```

### Extracting the best number of Neighbors

```{R}

#Extract the best number of neighbors (K)
best_knn_params <- select_best(knn_tune_results, metric = "roc_auc")

#Plug that best K back into the workflow
knn_final_workflow <- finalize_workflow(
  knn_workflow,
  best_knn_params
)

#Fit the finalized workflow to the testing data
knn_final_fit <- last_fit(
  knn_final_workflow,
  split = data_split
)

# View the final test metrics for the KNN model!
collect_metrics(knn_final_fit)

```

The tuned KNN model ultimately gave us an accuracy percentage of 70.1%,
an roc_auc of 0.780, and a brier_class of 0.198. A KNN model is not
linear, because instead of drawing a straight line, it looks at a play
and asks, "What happened in the 15 most similar historical situations?"
Because NFL play calling is highly situational, grouping similar plays
together works much better than a rigid, linear rule. KNN essentially
calculates "distance" between plays. But because we have a lot of
categorical dummy variables (like play_style_Aggressive or
play_style_Conservative), calculating mathematical "distance" ultimately
can get a little fuzzy. Which is where we now move into the tree models
to see how it will do there.

## Decision Tree Model:

Next model we are going to look at is a decision tree. A decision tree
builds a flowchart that we can visualize and it can give exact rules,
like: "If the play is aggressive, AND it's 4th down, AND there are more
than 5 yards to go, THEN predict a Catastrophic Failure." Instead of
doing complex geometry (like KNN) or drawing a single straight line
(like Logistic Regression), a Decision tree basically asks a series of
"If-Then" questions. For instance: "IF the play is Aggressive, and IF
there are less than 60 seconds left... THEN preidct Failure."

First we need to install the necessary packages to work with the tree
packages.

```{R}
library(rpart)
library(rpart.plot)

```

First let's look at a non-tuned decision tree model. When a non-tuned
version of this model is ran, R's `rpart` engine uses its default rules.
Basically it stops growing the tree if a bucket has fewer than 20 plays,
and it applies a standard complexity penalty of 0.01

```{R}
#Define the basic decision tree:
basic_tree_spec <- decision_tree() %>%
  set_engine("rpart") %>%
  set_mode("classification")

#Bundle into workflow (which includes our nfl_recipe)
basic_tree_workflow <- workflow() %>%
  add_recipe(nfl_recipe) %>%
  add_model(basic_tree_spec)

#Fit to resamples (cross-validation is utilized for basic models)
set.seed(2025) #Change to the same seed we used before for consistency
basic_tree_resamples <- fit_resamples(
  basic_tree_workflow,
  resamples = data_folds,
  metrics = metric_set(roc_auc, accuracy),
  control = control_resamples(save_pred = TRUE)
)


#Displaying the results
#We use collect_metrics() instead of show_best() since there's no grid of winners
collect_metrics(basic_tree_resamples)


```

### Extracting the basic decision tree

```{R}
# Pass the original workflow directly into last_fit
basic_tree_final_fit <- last_fit(
  basic_tree_workflow,
  split = data_split
)

# View the final test metrics for the basic decision tree!
collect_metrics(basic_tree_final_fit)

```

The non-tuned decision tree model ultimately gave us an accuracy
percentage of 66.6%, an ROC AUC score of 0.743, and a brier_class of
0.194. The decision tree beat both the logistic regression and elastic
net models because it can make conditional rules (if 4th quarter AND
down by 4, then Aggressive), unlike a single tree which is prone to
being a little too simplistic.

## Tuned Decision Tree:

Now let's try and run a tuning decision tree model.

`cost_complexity`: A math penalty that forces the tree to only add a new
branch if it significantly improves accuracy.

`tree_depth`: The absolute maximum number of levels the tree is allowed
to grow.

`min_n`: The minimum number of plays that must be in a group before the
tree is allowed to split it again

Over here is where we create our upgraded tuned decision tree model

```{R}
library(themis)
library(tidymodels)


#Defining the tuned decision tree model
tree_spec <- decision_tree(
  cost_complexity = tune(),
  tree_depth = tune()
) %>%
  set_engine("rpart") %>%
  set_mode("classification")

# Bundle into the workflow
tree_workflow <- workflow() %>%
  add_recipe(nfl_recipe) %>% 
  add_model(tree_spec)

#Creating the tuning grid
#We will test 25 different combinations of depth and complexity
tree_grid <- grid_regular(
  cost_complexity(),
  tree_depth(),
  levels = 10  #R tests 10 depths and 10 complexity penalties. 
  #10 * 10 = 100 total tree combinations, 500 total fits across the folds
)

#If levels = 30, then R tests 30 depths and 30 complexity penalties
# 30 * 30 = 900 total tree combinations. Across our 5 folds, R has to fit 4,500 models


#Tuning the model across our folds
set.seed(2025) #important for retaining the same value each time we run the model
tree_tune_results <- tune_grid(
  tree_workflow,
  resamples = data_folds,
  grid = tree_grid,
  metrics = metric_set(roc_auc, accuracy)
)

show_best(tree_tune_results, metric = "roc_auc")

```

### Extracting the tuned decision tree

```{R}

# Finalize the tuned tree!
best_tree_params <- select_best(tree_tune_results, metric = "roc_auc")

tree_final_workflow <- finalize_workflow(
  tree_workflow,
  best_tree_params
)

tree_final_fit <- last_fit(
  tree_final_workflow,
  split = data_split
)

collect_metrics(tree_final_fit)

```

Tuning definitely made such a difference in our decision tree. With our
tuned decision tree, it scored an accuracy percentage of 72.8% and an
ROC_AUC value of 0.795. By letting our model test 100 different
combinations, it figured out exactly how deep to grow its branches to
perfectly capture the context of the game without overfitting.

Decision trees are just notorious for over-fitting because they tend to
memorize the training data instead of learning the general rules. In a
sport that's as chaotic as football, a model that draws a single, rigid
mathematical line (logistic regression) often outperforms a model that
tries to build highly specific buckets for every scenario, which is what
the decision tree does.

It did better than the non-tuned decision tree model because the
non-tuned tree ultimately ended up with an ROC AUC mean of 0.795
compared to what the tuned decision tree's ROC AUC value was (which was
0.743).

### Decision Tree Plot:

```{r}
library(rpart)
library(rpart.plot)

#Extracting the underlying math from our workflow
tree_engine <- extract_fit_engine(tree_final_fit)

#Pruning the tree:
#We force it to be simpler just for the visualization
#The higher the 'cp' number, the more branches get chopped off.
# 0.015 is usually the sweet spot for a clean visual
presentation_tree <- rpart::prune(tree_engine, cp = 0.015)
#we explicitly call rpart::prune to prevent tidymodels from hijacking it



#Plotting the tree
rpart.plot(
  presentation_tree,
  main = "Decision Tree: Core Rules for Late-Game NFL Outcomes",
  type = 4,   #Draws clean branches
  extra = 104,  #Shows percentages inside the boxes
  box.palette = c("#FF4136", "#e0f3f8"), #Red for lost game, Blue for Won game
  shadow.col = "gray",
  nn = TRUE,
  tweak = 1.2 #This makes the text 20% larger
)

```

Looking at the visual of our decision tree that we created, the way we
read this, starting with the very top node (the root node), where it
asks, "How much time is left in the game?" The negative numbers (like
-0.58) likely means that our data was scaled or standardized somewhere
in the pipeline, so -0.58 just means "below average time remaining". If
the time remaining is below that threshold, the tree immediately drops
down the left branch and predicts a lost game with 79% confidence.

If a team survives that first split (meaning that they have enough time
left on the clock), the tree moves to the right and immediately asks the
next most important question: "What's the score" (`score_differential`).
If the score differential is high enough (\>= 1.3), it drops down to the
far right branch and predicts a Won Game with 82% confidence. But if the
score is closer, then it creates a few more complex splits based on
exact score margins.

### Decision Tree Tuning Performance:

As seen in our pruned decision tree, the foundational rules defining a
win or a loss rely strictly on game context (time and score).
Play-calling style however was not significant enough to be included in
the model's primary decision, since it's a secondary factor.

```{R}

library(ggplot2)

# Generate the autoplot for the Decision Tree tuning results
autoplot(tree_tune_results) +
  labs(
    title = "Decision Tree Tuning Performance",
    subtitle = "How Cost Complexity and Tree Depth affect Model Accuracy and AUC",
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

```

This model ultimately showcases our behind the scenes math of how our
decision tree learned to stop underfitting without accidentally crossing
the line into overfitting.

The bottom orange/brown lines represent very shallow trees (Tree Depth
of 1 or 2). They didn't do too hot because they are too simple
(underfitting). A tree with only one split can't possibly understand the
complexities of an NFL football game.

The lines grouped closely together at the very top (the purple, pink,
and light blue lines) represent deep trees (consists of a tree depth of
10, 11, 13, 15). This visually proves that predicting late-game play
calls requires a highly layered complex set of conditional rules.

Looking at the left side of this graph, the penalty is tiny (1e -08).
That means the tree is allowed to grow as wild and deep as it wants,
which is why performance is high and stable.

But looking at what happened when the penalty reaches (1e -02) on the
right side. Every single line falls off a massive cliff! When the
cost-complexity penalty got too strict, the algorithm essentially took a
chainsaw to our tree. It aggressively chopped off so many branches that
the deep trees were forced to become simple trees, destroying their
predictive power and causing the AUC to plummet.

## Random Forest Model:

Now our final model that we plan on creating is a random forest model:
Model tuning (also known as hyperparameter tuning) is what I attempted
to use for my random forest model. We would use this for a non default
setting. Tuning is the process of having the computer test dozens of
different settings (like how many variables the tree is allowed to look
at, or how deep the tree is allowed to grow) to find the absolute
mathematically optimal version of our model.

```{R}
# 1. Specify the Random Forest model
rf_spec <- rand_forest(
  mtry = tune(), 
  trees = tune(), 
  min_n = tune()
  ) %>%
  
  set_engine("ranger", importance = "impurity") %>%
  set_mode("classification")

# 2. Bundle the recipe and model into a workflow
rf_workflow <- workflow() %>%
  add_model(rf_spec) %>%
  add_recipe(nfl_recipe)

# 3. Set up a tuning grid (trying different combinations of mtry and min_n)
#While also including the trees range
rf_grid <- grid_regular(
  mtry(range = c(1, 5)), 
  trees(range = c(300,600)), 
  min_n(range = c(2, 10)),
  levels = 5
)

#This will take a while!
#Because we have 2 parameters, and levels = 5
#This means the grid is testing 5 * 5 * 5 = 125 different forest combinations
#Across our 5 cross-validation folds, the computer is actively building and grading 625 different models!

# Set seed for reproducibility
set.seed(2025)

```

Unlike a single decision tree, which is highly prone to overfitting to
specific game scenarios, a random forest model builds an entire "wisdom
of the crowd" ensemble. It generates hundreds of distinct decision trees
and aggregates their predictions, resulting in a highly robust and
mathematically stable model capable of navigating the unpredictable
nature of football!

`mtry:` (1 to 5): This controls how many random predictors the model is
allowed to look at before making a split. By forcing the trees to
occasionally ignore dominant variables (like the score differential or
the clock), the model is forced to uncover the hidden value in secondary
variables, such as our specific `play_style` calls.

`trees:` (300 to 600): This defines the sheer size of the forest. I
didn't want to expand my forest greater than this amount because going
for an infinitely large forest is computationally expensive and
mathematically unnecessary. Eventually the model will reach it's peak
performance and can no longer improve the Area Under the Curve (AUC).
Which was why I opted to test a tuning range of 300 to 600 trees.

`min_n:` (2 to 10: This dictates the minimum number of plays required in
a node before the tree is allowed to split again, acting as a natural
brake against overly complex, over-fitted rules.

```{R}

# Run the tuning process
rf_tune_results <- tune_grid(
  rf_workflow,
  resamples = data_folds,
  grid = rf_grid,
  metrics = metric_set(roc_auc, accuracy)
)

# View the results!
show_best(rf_tune_results, metric = "roc_auc")

```

```{R}
#Extract the best combination of mtry, min_n, and trees
best_rf_params <- select_best(rf_tune_results, metric = "roc_auc")

#Plug those perfect parameters back into the workflow
rf_final_workflow <- finalize_workflow(
  rf_workflow,
  best_rf_params
)

#Fit the finalized workflow to the testing data
rf_final_fit <- last_fit(
  rf_final_workflow,
  split = data_split
)

# 4. View the ultimate test metrics!
collect_metrics(rf_final_fit)

```

Looking at our ROC_AUC values for our tuned random forest model, we can
see that we get approximately a value of 0.835. This is the most
important metric for classification models, more specifically it
measures our model's ability to distinguish between a win and a loss.
Sports data in general can be notoriously noisy and unpredictable, so
building a model that score that high on completely unseen testing data
means that we found a very real, very strong signal in our data! Looks
like we found our winning model!

Having an ROC_AUC of 0.835 means that if we randomly select one
late-game situation where the team eventually won, and one where they
lost, our random forest will correctly identify the winning situation
83.5% of the time.

Looking at the accuracy score, out of the 431 plays within the unseen
testing dataset, our model correctly predicted the final outcome of the
game 78% of the time based purely on our predictors (`play_style`,
`yardline`, `score_differential`, etc).

A brier class measures the confidence of our model's predictions (where
0 is perfect and 0.25 is random guessing for balanced data). A brier
class score of 0.149 means that when our model predicts a win, it is
highly confident in that prediction, rather than just barely scraping by
with a 51% probability.

### ROC Curve: Random Forest

```{R}
library(vip)
library(ggplot2)

# PLOT THE ROC CURVE
roc_plot <- collect_predictions(rf_final_fit) %>%
  roc_curve(truth = won_game, `.pred_Won Game`, event_level = "second") %>% 
  # Change .pred_Win to .pred_Loss if your positive class is Loss
  # event_level = "second" to stop the curve from inverting
  autoplot() +
  labs(
    title = "ROC Curve: Random Forest",
    subtitle = "Final Test Data Performance (AUC: 0.835)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

print(roc_plot)
```

The dotted line is basically representing an AUC of 0.50 but the solid
black line is our random forest model. The fact that our curve bows so
far up toward the top-left corner is the visual proof of our outstanding
0.835 AUC score. Which ultimately shows how much "smarter" our model is
than just randomly guessing. In sports analytics, predicting outcomes is
notoriously difficult because games involve so much luck and human
error. What this ROC curve proves is that late-game NFL success isn't
just random luck, but our predictors (score_differential, yardline,
playstyle, etc.) contain a very real mathematically provable signal that
dictates who wins the game.

### Random Forest Plot

```{R}

library(ggplot2)

# Generate the autoplot for the Random Forest tuning results
autoplot(rf_tune_results, metric = "roc_auc") #+
#  labs(
#    title = "Random Forest Tuning Performance",
#    subtitle = "Optimizing Predictors (mtry), Tree Depth (min_n), and Number of Trees",
#    color = "Minimal Node Size (min_n)"
#  ) +
#  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
#  theme(
#    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
#    legend.position = "right"
#  )


```

Looking at this faceted plot, this graph is essentially the map that
proves exactly how our random forest reached that 0.835 AUC. The steep
climb of mtry (the x-axis). Our lines start way down near 0.75 when the
number of randomly selected predictors is at 1, but then shoot up
drastically when it moves to 2, 3, 4, and 5. What this means is if we
force the random forest to only look at 1 variable per split, it
performs terribly. It needs to look at a wider "menu" of variables (like
time, score, and yardline) simultaneously to make smart decisions about
NFL play calls.

The clustered lines are the number of trees. The different colored lines
appear to be tightly packed. Looking at the minimal node size 4, we can
see that our champion model is that olive green dot located at the far
right (olive green represents 375 trees). So this is when we have an
mtry = 5, trees = 375, and min_n = 4. It was the one that gave us that
outstanding ROC_AUC mean score of 0.7931060

This model is also really robust, because if we look at all five boxes,
they look very similar. No matter how much the min_n value range shifts,
the AUC stays within that 0.78 - 0.79 range.

### Variable Importance Plot:

```{R}

#Plotting the variable importance
vip_plot <- rf_final_fit %>%
  extract_fit_parsnip() %>%
  vip(geom = "col", num_features = 10, mapping = aes(fill = Variable)) +
  labs(
    title = "Random Forest: Variable Importance",
    subtitle = "Which factors most heavily influenced the game's outcome?",
    x = "Importance Score",
    y = "Predictor Variable"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

print(vip_plot)


```

From what we can see, `game_seconds_remaining`, `score_differential`,
and `yardline_100` are the ultimate predictors of whether a team wins or
loses. This makes a lot of sense because if my team is up by 7 points
with 30 seconds left on my opponent's 10 yard line, then my team is
going to win regardless of what play that I call. The situation dictates
the outcome far more than the play itself.

The plot twist though to all of this is that the play styles don't
really matter. There is no universally "good" or "bad" play style.
Aggressive play calling isn't inherently better than conservative play
calling. The success of the play style is entirely dependent on the "Big
Three" variables above it. What the random forest model determined for
us is it learned than a conservative play is a winning play if the time
and score are right, and a losing play if they aren't.

`ydstogo` and `down` sit firmly in the middle. They matter more than the
specific play call but they are still heavily overshadowed by how much
time is left on the clock and what the score is. `Catastrophic_failure`
is surprisingly really low though, considering a fumble or an
interception usually ruins a game. But in machine learning, variable
importance measures how useful a variable is across all rows of data.
Because catastrophic failures are rare (they tend to not happen on every
single play), the model relies much more heavily on variables that exist
on every single play (like the clock and the yardline) to make its
overall predictions.

### Random Forest Confusion Matrix:

```{R}

# Generate a Confusion Matrix for the champion Random Forest
rf_final_fit %>%
  collect_predictions() %>%
  conf_mat(truth = won_game, estimate = .pred_class) %>%
  autoplot(type = "heatmap") +
  labs(
    title = "Confusion Matrix: Random Forest",
    subtitle = "Which outcomes are the hardest to predict?"
  )

```

This model in particular is really good at identifying losing situations
(258). When it sees a team that is likely to lose, then it's almost
always right. The model also successfully identified 79 winning
situations. The bottom left corner indicates false wins (50). These are
50 plays where the model predicted a "Win", but the team actually lost.
In football terms, these are likely the "heartbreak" games or the teams
that had such a high probability of winning on the score and time, but
absolutely blew it (because of maybe costly turnovers and missed field
goals). The top right corner identifies false losses (44). These are 44
plays where the model predicted a "Loss", but the team actually won.
These are essentially the "miracle" games or the incredible comebacks
that defied the typical statistical rules of the game.

The Confusion Matrix reveals that while the model is highly proficient
at identifying clear losing scenarios (84% accuracy), it is more
challenged by winning scenarios (64%). This suggests that late game NFL
wins often involve 'miracle' outliers or unpredictable shifts that fall
outside the standard patterns learned by the random forest, whereas
losing paths are more statistically predictable.

# THE ULTIMATE TEST!!!

![](images/49ers%20players%20huddling%20up-01.webp)

Imagine this: It's the two-minute warning
(`game_seconds_remaining = 120`). Your team is down by exactly a field
goal (`score_differential = -3`). You have the ball right at midfield
(`yardline_100 = 50`) on 1st and 10 (`down = 1, ydstogo = 10`). The home
crowd is electric, the season is on the line! What type of play do you
call??

