```{R}
library(tidymodels)


#Fit the finalized workflow to our clean training data
deployment_model <- fit(rf_final_workflow, data = train_data_clean)



# 1. Create the exact scenarios
# We use c() to create two identical rows, but flip the play style
hypothetical_scenarios <- tibble(
  score_differential = c(-3, -3), #3 point deficit
  game_seconds_remaining = c(120, 120), #120 seconds remaining
  ydstogo = c(10, 10), #10 yards to go
  yardline_100 = c(50, 50), #at 50 yard line
  down = c(1, 1), #on a first down
  
  # Passing "0" as a string prevents R from getting confused about factor levels
  catastrophic_failure = factor(c("0", "0"), levels = c("0", "1")),
  
  # The recipe's step_string2factor will handle this for us automatically now!
  play_style = c("Aggressive", "Conservative") 
)


#Predict the probabilities

# We use type = "prob" to get the actual percentages
win_probabilities <- predict(deployment_model, new_data = hypothetical_scenarios, type = "prob")

# 4. Bind the results together
final_results <- bind_cols(hypothetical_scenarios, win_probabilities)

# 5. View the final side-by-side comparison!
final_results %>%
  select(play_style, starts_with(".pred_Won"))


```

Here are the results!! If you went for an aggressive play, you would
have a 5.4% chance of winning the game. Attempting a "hero play" (an
immediate long pass for a TD for instance) is really risky, but really
epic if done successfully. Technically speaking, when that team only
needs a field goal to tie the game because they are down by 3, there is
no reason for them to go for an aggressive pass, especially if it's
first down.

But if you chose conservative, then you are great at calling plays! The
model predicts a 34.8% win probability. This is the right choice in this
situation, because you're only down by 3, and it's first down. I would
opt for a run play to try and get extra yardage.

## New Scenario!

Now we have cranked the pressure up to a terrifying level! There is now
only one minute left on the clock (`game_seconds_remaining = 120`). You
are still down by 3 points (`score_differential = -3`). You have driven
down to the opponent's 20 yard line (`yardline_100 = 20`), but the drive
has stalled. It is 4th and 5! (`down = 4, ydstogo = 5`). If you don't
convert, the game is offically over! Do you go for it, or kick the field
goal to take the points (which could be considered conservative)?

```{R}


# 1. Create the NEW High-Pressure Scenario
hypothetical_scenarios_2 <- tibble(
  score_differential = c(-3, -3),        # Still down by 3 points
  game_seconds_remaining = c(60, 60),    # ONLY 1 MINUTE LEFT!
  ydstogo = c(5, 5),                     # 5 yards to get the 1st down
  yardline_100 = c(20, 20),              # On the opponent's 20-yard line (Red Zone)
  down = c(4, 4),                        # 4TH DOWN (Do or Die!)
  
  # These stay exactly the same for the model to work
  catastrophic_failure = factor(c("0", "0"), levels = c("0", "1")),
  play_style = c("Aggressive", "Conservative") 
)

# 2. Predict the probabilities using your deployed model!
win_probabilities_2 <- predict(deployment_model, new_data = hypothetical_scenarios_2, type = "prob")

# 3. Bind the results together
final_results_2 <- bind_cols(hypothetical_scenarios_2, win_probabilities_2)

# 4. View the final side-by-side comparison!
final_results_2 %>%
  select(play_style, down, ydstogo, starts_with(".pred_Won"))


```

Even with the situation of do or die, our random forest model still
firmly believes that a conservative play call (\~35.9% win probability)
is significantly better than an aggressive play call (\~27.7% win
probability)! For a conservative play case, a coach can call a quick
5-yard slant, a check-down, or a gritty run play to simply get the first
down and move the chains. But if going aggressive, then that coach
really wants to score a touchdown, even though they can get a first down
five yards away, but they have to be mindful that mistakes are more
likely with a play like this.

## Final Scenario

Now finally, let's say we have a third and long scenario
(`down = 3, ydstogo = 11`). The situation is dire, your team is down by
a full touchdown (`score_differential = -7`) with a mere 45 seconds
remaining (`game_seconds_remaining = 45`). What would you do??

```{R}

# 1. "3rd and Long" Desperation Scenario
hypothetical_scenarios_3 <- tibble(
  score_differential = c(-7, -7),        # Down by a full touchdown (7 points)
  game_seconds_remaining = c(45, 45),    # Only 45 seconds left on the clock
  ydstogo = c(11, 11),                   # 11 yards needed to move the chains
  yardline_100 = c(35, 35),              # On the opponent's 35-yard line (Fringe Field Goal Range)
  down = c(3, 3),                        # 3RD DOWN
  
  # These stay exactly the same for the model to work
  catastrophic_failure = factor(c("0", "0"), levels = c("0", "1")),
  play_style = c("Aggressive", "Conservative") 
)

# 2. Predict the probabilities using your deployed model
win_probabilities_3 <- predict(deployment_model, new_data = hypothetical_scenarios_3, type = "prob")

# 3. Bind the results together
final_results_3 <- bind_cols(hypothetical_scenarios_3, win_probabilities_3)

# 4. View the final side-by-side comparison!
final_results_3 %>%
  select(play_style, down, ydstogo, starts_with(".pred_Won"))



```

What our model predicts is 6.2% for an aggressive play call and 18% for
a conservative play call. Now this one is interesting to talk about.
Even though your team is so far away from the first down line to keep
the drive alive and even though the time on the clock is really low, it
is way more smarter to not go for a hero ball and to try and go for a
12-yard pass designed to purely get that first down.

This model mathematically confirms that even when trailing by a full
touchdown with under a minute to play, securing the immediate first down
is statistically more vital to comeback than attempting a low-percentage
immediate pass for the end zone.

# Model Results

After testing a diverse gauntlet of machine learning models, we have
came to a conclusion that the **random forest** model has been crowned
the champion!

*Linear models* (logistic regression & elastic net) severely underfitted
the data, with ROC AUC scores between 0.66 and 0.71. These models
attempt to draw a rigid, straight line through the data. And for a sport
like American football that involves so many unpredictable play calls,
linear models simply could not grasp that nuance.

*K-Nearest Neighbors* (KNN) is a distance-based model that greatly
improved the performance to an ROC AUC score of 0.780, as the model
succeeded by finding historically similar plays for the outcome. This
proved that situation and context matter more than isolated variables.

*Decision Trees* excelled at mapping out the "If-Then" conditional logic
of football. While the basic nontuned tree performed admirably, our
hyperparameter tuning process significantly boosted its predictive power
with an ROC AUC score of 0.795. Which allowed the model to find the
perfect depth without overfitting.

*Random Forest* however, was the ultimate champion model! By aggregating
the predictions of 375 distinct decision trees, the random forest
eliminated the variance of a single tree and perfectly captured the
overlapping realities of NFL strategy. It achieved a high ROC AUC score
of 0.835 and an accuracy of 78%, proving it was highly capable of
distinguishing between game-winning and game-losing situations on
completely unseen data.

# Conclusion

So to conclude, we sought to answer one of the most debated questions in
football strategy: in high leverage situations, is conservative or
aggressive play calling more successful in winning big games? By
analyzing play-by-play data and building a robust machine learning
pipeline, we moved past coaching intuition and let the math dictate the
answer.

NFL fans (and often coaches) assume that going for it on 4th down
requires a high-risk aggressive "hero play" (like a deep pass to the
endzone). But our model proved that even when a team needs 5 yards to
get a first down to keep the game alive, throwing a high-risk deep pass
actually drops your chances of winning by over 8%. The model essentially
favors the "boring" 6-yard slant or the safe check-down because
protecting the football and simply moving the chains is always more
valuable than gambling on a miracle. Therefore I can fairly say, a
conservative play calling strategy is more often than not the way to go,
but that doesn't mean you don't have to go for a hero ball to win it
all! Just ask Caleb Williams!

And just for your guys' pleasure, here is the ultimate definition of a
"hero-ball" to win a football game! Caleb Williams, quarterback of the
Chicago Bears, throws a 46 yard dart to DJ Moore who makes an incredible
catch and they won the game in overtime over the Green Bay Packers!

![](images/Caleb%20Williams%20TD%20pass%20vs%20Packers.gif)

Overall, I really had a lot of fun putting together this project! The
challenges were definitely there, but when my models were finally able
to run, it was the most satisfying thing ever! I have always had a huge
passion for sports, especially for football, and I always wanted to do
something like this. I really learned a lot about the different models I
can use to predict. My favorite part was putting together visualizations
of the data in the EDA section, and seeing the beautifully plotted data
in a boxplot, barplot, density plots, you name it! I'm definitely
looking forward to doing more projects like this in the future, and am
really excited about what the future of sports analytics has for us!
